author: _shemnon
content: I've been messing around locally on a local project I have for transactions.  While SSZ micro-benchmarks faster for encode/decode the hash-from-zero is slower in SSZ (probably because of the trie) but clever coding practices can make this a one-time hit. But for an "all in" test we saw a slgith advantage to RLP, although this may be related to non-serialization tasks.  However, SSZ's ability to do partial proofs has no efficient analogue in RLP. Wether or not this is useful the engine api it will be critical in other parts of the EL protocols.

author: potuz
content: how are you hashing from zero?

author: funnygiulio
content: hashing is not useful for engine api. and this is weird. hashing in ssz can be easily parallelized. in rlp it cannot

author: _shemnon
content: "from zero" I mean I have never seen the data before. (like it's a new block w/o mempool)

author: _shemnon
content: Partial proofs are the killer feature, I'm just not sure how the engine API would need it.

author: potuz
content: I ask how do you actually coded this hashing from zero. I think if you hash correctly even cold hashing, you SSZ should be probably faster or at par.  Using <https://github.com/OffchainLabs/hashtree> and <https://hackmd.io/80mJ75A5QeeRcrNmqcuU-g>

author: potuz
content: SHA256 has this native parallelization that keccack does not have

author: funnygiulio
content: But also rlp hashing is not parallelizable

author: funnygiulio
content: no but like what I am saying is that this is not even a number's game because we don't know each theoretical best implementations of each algo looks like. ssz should be faster because even its decoding can be parallelized, but showing a bunch of benchmarks on a bunch of libraries does not help either case. ssz is more hashing friendly (reason why it is the choice for CL) and its the new encoding for Ethereum so that's what we should use. current impl shows SSZ is 2x faster than RLP so even better than but this is not entirely an argument on performance
